After you activate tags in the Billing console, AWS generates a cost allocation report with usage and costs grouped by your active tags. You can use AWS-generated tags (like aws:createdBy) or user-defined tags to categorize costs by project, department, or environment.

QuestionText: Which of the following are benefits of using Amazon S3? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, meaning your data is extremely unlikely to be lost. S3 also provides virtually unlimited storage capacity—you can store as many objects as you want, and individual objects can be up to 5 TB. These characteristics make S3 ideal for backup, archiving, data lakes, and content distribution.
Option3: Built-in compute processing for running applications
Option4: Automatic code deployment to EC2 instances

QuestionText: Which of the following are features of Amazon VPC? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Amazon VPC allows you to create subnets to segment your network into public-facing and private sections. Public subnets have routes to the internet via an Internet Gateway, while private subnets do not. VPC also allows you to define route tables that control how traffic is directed between subnets, to the internet, or to VPN connections.
Option1: Creation of public and private subnets
Option2: Custom route tables to control traffic flow

QuestionText: Which of the following are benefits of using AWS CloudTrail? (Select TWO)
Explanation: CloudTrail provides a complete event history of API calls made in your AWS account, which is essential for governance and compliance auditing—you can prove who did what and when. It also enables security analysis by recording the source IP, identity, and timestamp of every API call, helping detect unauthorized access or suspicious activity patterns.
Option1: Governance and compliance auditing of all API activity
Option2: Security analysis through detailed API call logging
Option4: Real-time blocking of unauthorized API calls

QuestionText: Which of the following are characteristics of AWS Regions? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Each AWS Region is a separate geographic area that operates independently from other Regions. Data is not automatically replicated between Regions—you must explicitly configure cross-Region replication if needed. Each Region also contains multiple Availability Zones (at least 3 in most Regions), providing redundancy and fault isolation within the Region.
Option1: Each Region is geographically isolated and operates independently
Option2: Each Region contains multiple Availability Zones
Option3: Data is automatically replicated across all Regions
Option4: All Regions have identical service offerings

QuestionText: Which of the following are features of the AWS Business Support plan? (Select TWO)
Explanation: The Business Support plan provides 24/7 access to Cloud Support Engineers via phone, email, and chat for technical issues. It also includes access to the full set of AWS Trusted Advisor checks (all categories: cost optimization, security, fault tolerance, performance, and service limits), whereas Basic and Developer plans only include core Trusted Advisor checks.
Option1: 24/7 access to Cloud Support Engineers via phone email and chat
Option2: Full set of AWS Trusted Advisor checks
Option4: Response time of less than 15 minutes for critical issues

QuestionText: Which of the following are components of the AWS Shared Responsibility Model that are ALWAYS the customer's responsibility regardless of the service used? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Regardless of whether you use EC2 (IaaS), RDS (managed), or Lambda (serverless), the customer is always responsible for managing their data—including what data they store, how it is classified, and who can access it. Customer identity and access management (IAM configuration) is also always the customer's responsibility, as AWS cannot determine who should have access to your resources.
Option3: Physical security of AWS data centers

QuestionText: Which of the following BEST describes the concept of scalability in AWS? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Vertical scaling (scaling up) means increasing the size of an individual resource—for example, upgrading an EC2 instance from t3.medium to t3.xlarge to get more CPU and memory. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more resources of the same type—for example, adding more EC2 instances behind a load balancer to handle increased traffic. Both are forms of scalability.
Option1: Vertical scaling by increasing the size of individual resources
Option2: Horizontal scaling by adding more resources of the same type
Option3: Scaling by moving to a different AWS Region
Option4: Scaling by purchasing more AWS support

QuestionText: Which of the following statements about AWS pricing are TRUE? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS follows a pay-as-you-go model where you pay only for what you consume with no upfront costs for most services. Additionally, many AWS services offer tiered pricing where the per-unit cost decreases as usage increases—for example, S3 storage costs less per GB at higher usage tiers. These pricing models help organizations optimize costs as they scale.
Option1: You pay only for what you consume with no upfront costs for most services
Option2: Many services offer tiered pricing where per-unit cost decreases with higher usage
Option3: All AWS services require a minimum 1-year commitment
Option4: AWS pricing is the same regardless of the Region you use

QuestionText: Which of the following are benefits of using AWS managed services like Amazon RDS instead of self-managing a database on EC2? (Select TWO)
Explanation: With managed services like RDS, AWS handles automated backups, including point-in-time recovery, so you don't need to manage backup scripts or storage. AWS also handles database software patching—applying security patches and minor version upgrades during maintenance windows you define. This reduces operational overhead and lets you focus on your application rather than database administration.
Option3: Complete control over the underlying operating system
Option4: Ability to install custom database engines not supported by RDS

QuestionText: Which of the following are characteristics of Amazon DynamoDB? (Select TWO)
Explanation: DynamoDB provides single-digit millisecond performance at any scale, making it suitable for applications requiring consistent low-latency data access. It also offers automatic scaling—DynamoDB can automatically adjust read and write throughput capacity based on actual traffic patterns, so you don't need to manually provision capacity or worry about performance degradation during traffic spikes.
Option1: Single-digit millisecond performance at any scale
Option2: Automatic scaling of read and write capacity
Option3: Support for complex SQL JOIN operations across tables
Option4: Requires manual capacity provisioning and cannot auto-scale

QuestionText: Which of the following are ways to protect data at rest in AWS? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Server-side encryption with AWS KMS managed keys (SSE-KMS) encrypts data at rest using keys managed in AWS Key Management Service, providing centralized key management and audit trails. Amazon S3 default encryption automatically encrypts all new objects stored in a bucket without requiring changes to your application. Both protect data at rest from unauthorized access.
Option1: Server-side encryption using AWS KMS managed keys
Option2: Enabling default encryption on Amazon S3 buckets
Option3: Using security groups to block network traffic
